What We Monitor / Transmissions

Asset type

Transmission monitoring

Belt drives, gearboxes, chain drives, and couplings each fail differently — and respond differently to monitoring technologies. The challenge is matching the right technology to the right transmission type. No single method covers them all.

Talk to an engineer See what we detect

Why transmissions are hard to monitor

The most diverse failure landscape in any drivetrain

Transmissions span the widest range of mechanical configurations in industrial systems. Each type has its own dominant failure modes, signal characteristics, and monitoring blind spots. Treating them as one category leads to missed faults and false confidence.

76%

of gearbox failures originate in bearings — not gears. Lubrication inefficiency is the root cause in over 60% of those bearing failures. (NREL Gearbox Reliability Database, 750 records)

~80%

of machine wear is particle-induced. Contamination particles as small as 5 μm act as abrasives inside gearboxes and bearings. Oil cleanliness determines gear life more than load does.

6 types

of transmission — belt drives, single-stage gearboxes, multi-stage gearboxes, planetary gearboxes, chain drives, and couplings — each with fundamentally different failure physics and monitoring requirements.

How ESA works on transmissions

The motor as transducer: reading mechanical faults through electrical current

Any mechanical event that modulates the torque seen by the motor creates sidebands in the stator current spectrum. The motor converts torque fluctuations into speed fluctuations, which change the back-EMF, which modulate the current. ESA reads these modulations from the motor control cabinet.

Motor inertia as low-pass filter

The motor rotor’s moment of inertia determines the mechanical bandwidth. High-frequency torque fluctuations are smoothed before they reach the electrical signature. Low-frequency fluctuations pass through. This single physics principle explains why ESA detects some transmission faults and misses others.

Belt-pass frequency: ESA’s sweet spot

Belt faults produce torque modulation at the belt-pass frequency, which is always sub-synchronous — below motor running speed. This sits well below the motor inertia cutoff and transmits efficiently through the motor with minimal attenuation, regardless of motor size. Belt drives are ESA’s strongest transmission application. (IEEE 2022, MDPI 2023 — peer-reviewed)

Gear mesh frequency: motor-size dependent

Gear mesh frequency = teeth × shaft RPM. For most gearboxes, this is super-synchronous. Motor inertia attenuates it. Detection proven at 30 kW (Combet 2015), fails at 2 MW. Practical limit: motors ≤200 kW with single-stage gearboxes where mesh frequency stays below ~200 Hz.

The scaling rule

Motor inertia scales approximately as J ∝ P5/4. As motors get larger, the mechanical cutoff frequency drops. Small motors (≤30 kW) can detect gear mesh up to ~200 Hz. Medium motors (30–200 kW) become unreliable above ~100 Hz. Large motors (>500 kW) pass only sub-synchronous frequencies. At 2 MW, even single-stage gear mesh detection fails. This boundary is structural — not a software limitation.

What ESA detects on transmissions

Detection capability varies dramatically by transmission type

This is the most important section on this page. ESA’s effectiveness depends on the signal path between the fault and the motor. Belt drives sit in ESA’s sweet spot. Multi-stage gearboxes sit in its weakest zone.

Belt drives

Sub-synchronous frequencies. ESA’s strongest transmission application. Peer-reviewed evidence (IEEE 2022, MDPI 2023).

Fault Confidence How ESA detects it Conditions
Belt wear & deterioration Full Belt-pass frequency amplitude changes in demodulated current spectrum All motor sizes
Belt tension loss Full Belt-pass frequency shift and amplitude change All motor sizes
Belt misalignment Full Asymmetric belt-pass harmonics All motor sizes
Sheave / pulley wear Full Running speed modulation, belt-pass changes All motor sizes
Belt slip / glazing Full Speed variation and efficiency loss signature Continuous monitoring advantage over periodic inspection

Single-stage gearboxes

Moderate capability on small-to-medium motors. Proven at 30 kW (Combet 2015, peer-reviewed). Degrades with motor size.

Fault Confidence How ESA detects it Conditions
Gear tooth pitting (early) Conditional Gear mesh frequency sidebands in current spectrum Motor ≤200 kW. Mesh frequency <200 Hz. Requires advanced signal processing.
Gear tooth breakage Full Sudden torque spike and load change Any motor size. Catastrophic event detection.
Input shaft bearing Conditional BPFO sidebands in current spectrum Motor ≤100 kW preferred. Short signal path helps. Low-to-moderate confidence.
Output shaft bearing Conditional Signal must traverse gear mesh + coupling Typically detects at Stage 3–4 only. Vibration detects at Stage 1–2.
Shaft misalignment Full 2× running speed component in current All motor sizes. Well-established ESA signature.
Lubrication degradation None No electrical signature Oil analysis required. Invisible to ESA.

Multi-stage & planetary gearboxes

ESA’s weakest zone. Each stage adds inertia and compliance, attenuating fault signatures before they reach the motor. No validated gearbox CIs in the past 18 months (SAM4 Detection Accuracy Report, March 2026).

Fault Confidence How ESA detects it Conditions
High-speed stage gear faults Conditional Gear mesh sidebands from stage closest to motor Motor ≤200 kW. First stage only realistic. Low-to-moderate confidence.
Intermediate / low-speed stage faults None to minimal Signal attenuated through multiple stages Below practical detection threshold. Motor inertia + multi-stage attenuation.
Intermediate & output bearings None Maximum signal attenuation path No published industrial success. Vibration + oil analysis required.
Planetary gear / bearing faults None Distributed load paths average out individual fault signals Not detectable via ESA. Vibration + oil analysis are primary methods.
Overall load / efficiency change Full Power consumption trending All motor sizes. Detects degradation, not specific fault location.
Motor-gearbox misalignment Full 2× running speed in current All motor sizes. Strong ESA application.

Chain drives & couplings

Chain drives have no published ESA detection evidence. Couplings are mixed — misalignment is strong, but fluid couplings are a hard boundary.

Fault Confidence How ESA detects it Conditions
Chain elongation / wear Not validated No published evidence Chain-pass frequencies are super-synchronous. Motor inertia filters them. No ESA research exists.
Sprocket wear / link failure Not validated No published evidence Broadband noise, not clean spectral peaks. Visual inspection remains primary.
Coupling misalignment Full 2× running speed in current spectrum All motor sizes. All coupling types (except downstream of fluid couplings).
Elastomer degradation Conditional Torsional natural frequency shift Limited field evidence. Detectable as system-level change.
Faults downstream of fluid coupling Hard boundary No mechanical signal propagation through fluid medium Absolute ESA limitation. Fluid couplings block all mechanical information. By design.

Confidence ratings based on Combet & Gelman (2015), Blodt et al., Benbouzid, IEEE (2022), MDPI (2023), NREL Gearbox Reliability Database (750 records), and SAM4 Detection Accuracy Report (March 2026 — 0 gearbox CIs in 18-month window). See ESA detection constraints.

Detection boundaries

No condition monitoring technology detects everything. These are the known limitations for ESA-based monitoring on transmission systems — stated here because engineers need them to make informed decisions.

Fluid couplings: hard boundary. Fluid couplings transmit torque through hydraulic fluid, not mechanical contact. High-frequency mechanical signals dissipate as heat. ESA cannot detect any fault downstream of a fluid coupling. This is an absolute limitation, not a sensitivity one. Prevalent in conveyors, crushers, and large centrifugal equipment (estimated 20–40% of large drives in mining and cement).
Motor size scaling boundary. Gear mesh detection is proven at 30 kW (Combet 2015, peer-reviewed, industrial validation). At 2 MW, detection fails. Practical rule: ESA gear mesh detection is viable only for motors ≤200 kW with single-stage gearboxes where mesh frequency is below ~200 Hz. This boundary is structural — set by rotor inertia physics, not by software.
Multi-stage gearbox attenuation. Each gear mesh interface adds impedance mismatch. Each intermediate shaft adds inertia. The signal path: fault location → intermediate shafts → coupling → motor shaft → electrical signature. For gearboxes with 3+ stages, intermediate and output-stage faults fall below the ESA noise floor.
Planetary gearbox limitations. Planet gears mesh simultaneously with sun and ring gears, creating complex distributed load paths that average out individual fault signals. High inertia damps torque fluctuations before they reach the motor. ESA adds marginal value beyond motor health and gross load trending. Vibration + oil analysis are the primary monitoring methods for planetary gearboxes.
Gearbox bearing detection staging. ESA typically requires bearing faults to reach Stage 3–4 (advanced degradation with significant material loss) before detection. Vibration envelope analysis detects at Stage 1–2. Oil analysis detects wear particle generation even earlier. ESA is not a substitute for vibration or oil analysis on gearbox bearings.
Chain drives: not validated. No published ESA research on chain fault detection exists after extensive literature search. Chain-pass frequencies are super-synchronous, subject to motor inertia filtering. Chain impacts produce broadband noise rather than clean spectral peaks. Evidence is insufficient to make detection claims.
Oil-related failures: invisible to ESA. Contamination, oil degradation, additive depletion, and progressive wear from lubrication failure produce no direct electrical signature. Oil analysis detects threatening wear conditions up to 15× earlier than vibration (Monash University). For lubricated gearboxes, oil analysis is irreplaceable — neither ESA nor vibration can substitute.
Low-load operation (<30% rated). Fault sidebands in the current signal are proportional to mechanical load. Below ~30% of rated load, fault signatures weaken and may fall below the detection threshold.
Gearbox detection accuracy: low historical maturity. The SAM4 Detection Accuracy Report (March 2026) recorded zero gearbox CIs in the 18-month analysis window. Historical all-time data shows ~6 CIs with a 33–50% miss rate. This is insufficient to support standalone gearbox detection claims. Gearbox diagnostics should be positioned as part of drive-train analysis, not as a distinct capability.

Based on SAM4 Detection Accuracy Report (March 2026), Combet & Gelman (2015), NREL Gearbox Reliability Database, Monash University oil analysis research, and peer-reviewed ESA literature. See full detection transparency report →

Monitoring architecture

ESA, vibration, and oil analysis: three technologies, distinct roles

Transmissions are the asset type where ESA and vibration have the most distinct roles — and where oil analysis fills gaps neither technology reaches. Each method sees different fault types first. The monitoring question is coverage, not competition.

ESA leads
  • Belt slip, wear, tension loss, and misalignment (sub-synchronous, all motor sizes)
  • Motor electrical faults — stator winding, rotor bar, supply quality
  • Load anomalies, overload trending, efficiency loss
  • Fleet-scale screening from MCC — no per-unit sensor cost

ESA’s strongest transmission application is belt drives, where belt-pass frequency transmits efficiently through the motor regardless of motor size.

Both contribute
  • Gear tooth damage (vibration detects earlier; ESA confirms torque impact)
  • Coupling misalignment (ESA detects modulation; vibration provides phase-based root cause)
  • Overall drivetrain degradation — ESA sees load changes, vibration sees structural changes

When a fault appears in both spectra, it confirms the defect is affecting mechanical integrity. This reduces diagnostic uncertainty.

Vibration + oil analysis lead
  • Gearbox bearing faults — 76% of all gearbox failures (envelope analysis at Stage 1–2)
  • Early localised tooth damage — pitting, micro-cracking (TSA)
  • Intermediate/output stage faults in multi-stage gearboxes
  • Oil contamination, degradation, progressive wear (oil analysis detects 15× earlier)
  • All faults downstream of fluid couplings

Vibration has ~50 years of field-validated maturity on gearboxes. Oil analysis catches lubrication-driven failures that neither ESA nor vibration can see early enough.

Optimal monitoring architecture by transmission type

The right monitoring stack depends on the transmission type. For belt drives, ESA is primary. For multi-stage and planetary gearboxes, vibration and oil analysis are primary. ESA fills gaps on the assets that would otherwise go unmonitored.

Transmission type ESA role Primary limitation Best combined approach
Belt drives Primary. Belt-pass frequency, wear, tension, alignment — fleet-wide from MCC. Sheave groove wear has lower specificity. ESA for fleet monitoring. Periodic laser alignment + tension measurement on critical units.
Single-stage gearboxes Co-primary (≤200 kW). Motor health, load trending, first-stage gear/bearing. Motor inertia filters gear mesh on larger motors. Proven at 30 kW, fails at 2 MW. ESA + vibration. Oil analysis monthly–quarterly on critical units.
Multi-stage gearboxes Secondary. Motor health, efficiency trending, misalignment only. Cannot isolate gear stage. Bearings (76% of failures) not reliably visible. Vibration (TSA + envelope) primary. Oil analysis for progressive wear. ESA for motor condition.
Planetary gearboxes Tertiary. Motor health and gross load trending only. Complex distributed load paths. Planet bearing failures invisible to ESA. Vibration + oil analysis essential. ESA adds motor electrical monitoring only.
Couplings (mechanical) Complementary. Misalignment is a strong ESA application. Cannot distinguish misalignment from unbalance. Fluid couplings: hard boundary. Vibration for root-cause discrimination. ESA for trending and confirmation.
Chain drives Not recommended. Evidence insufficient. No published MCSA research. Super-synchronous frequencies filtered by motor inertia. Visual inspection + elongation measurement. Vibration on critical units.

Architecture recommendations based on Combet (2015), NREL Gearbox Reliability Database (750 records), Monash University oil analysis research, ISO 10816/13373, and SAM4 field deployment data.

ESA expands the monitored population, not the diagnostic ceiling

The strongest argument for ESA on transmissions is not diagnostic superiority — it is deployability at scale. Comprehensive vibration monitoring on a three-stage gearbox requires 12–16 accelerometers. Oil analysis requires sampling logistics and lab turnaround. ESA requires one sensor set at the MCC.

For a facility with 50–100 belt-driven or geared assets, ESA brings continuous condition data to enclosed gearbox packages, hazardous-area equipment, and belt-driven fleet assets that would otherwise go unmonitored. For critical multi-stage gearboxes, it complements but does not replace vibration and oil analysis. For belt-driven equipment fleets, it offers the best cost-to-coverage ratio of any single technology.

On complex gearboxes where ESA sensitivity is limited, detecting an anomaly still beats discovering a failure. Classification can follow from inspection.

Transmission detections

Real faults caught in transmission systems

Belt wear Pulp & Paper

Belt slip detected on cooling fan drive

Belt-pass frequency signatures in the current spectrum indicated progressive belt wear and tension loss. Belt replaced during planned maintenance before complete loss of drive torque.

Read detection story →
Misalignment Metals & Mining

Gearbox input misalignment on conveyor drive

Progressive 2× running speed rise in the current signature flagged increasing misalignment between motor and gearbox input shaft. Corrected before bearing damage progressed.

Read detection story →
Coupling fault Chemicals

Elastomeric coupling degradation on pump drive

Torsional natural frequency shift and increased backlash signature identified through characteristic frequency changes. Coupling replaced before shaft damage.

Read detection story →
Load anomaly Water & Wastewater

Drivetrain efficiency loss on aeration blower

Power consumption trending showed progressive efficiency loss on belt-driven blower. Investigation revealed belt tension degradation and sheave wear. Preventive replacement avoided unplanned blower shutdown.

Read detection story →
See all detection stories →

Installation on transmission systems

Under 60 minutes. No transmission access required.

1

Open the motor control cabinet

SAM4 installs at the MCC — the same panel your electricians already access. No access to the gearbox, coupling, or belt drive required. Back online same shift, no process interruption.

2

Clip sensor onto motor supply cables

Current and voltage sensors clip directly onto existing cabling. No wiring changes. No interruption to the transmission’s operation. This is why ESA scales to fleet-size deployments — the same installation covers belts, gearboxes, and couplings in the drivetrain.

3

Connect and commission

The SAM4 gateway connects via cellular (4G/LTE). No dependency on your IT network. Monitoring starts immediately, with first diagnostic results within 48 hours.

Full installation guide → Security & network architecture →

Related asset types

Other asset types SAM4 monitors

Fans & Blowers

Belt-driven fans are ESA’s strongest fleet-scale application. Ventilation, cooling, and process air.

Conveyors

Belt, screw, and chain conveyors in materials handling. Motor + gearbox health on distributed systems.

Pumps

Centrifugal, submersible, borehole, and canned motor pumps. Broadest fault detection portfolio.

Agitators & Mixers

Geared drives in chemical reactors, blending vessels, and mixing tanks. Variable load baseline challenge.
See all asset types →
